[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Removable+media%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Removable+media%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Removable+media%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Removable+media%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(September 2014)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

is a [data storage](/wiki/Computer_data_storage "Computer data storage") [media](/wiki/Storage_media "Storage media") that is designed to be readily inserted and removed from a system.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Most early removable media, such as [floppy disks](/wiki/Floppy_disk "Floppy disk") and [optical discs](/wiki/Optical_disc "Optical disc"), require a dedicated [read](/wiki/Reading_(computer) "Reading (computer)")/write device (i.e. a [drive](/wiki/Disk_drive "Disk drive")) to be installed in the computer,[[2]](#cite_note-2) while others, such as [USB flash drives](/wiki/USB_flash_drive "USB flash drive"), are [plug-and-play](/wiki/Plug-and-play "Plug-and-play") with all the hardware required to read them built into the device, so only need a [driver software](/wiki/Driver_software "Driver software") to be installed in order to communicate with the device.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Some removable media readers/drives are integrated into the [computer case](/wiki/Computer_case "Computer case"), while others are standalone devices that need to be additionally installed or connected.

["Expandable storage- Glossary"](https://csrc.nist.gov/glossary/term/removable-media). *CSRC*. NIST. [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20190708181935/https://csrc.nist.gov/glossary/term/removable-media) from the original on 8 July 2019. Retrieved 8 July 2019.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Exchangeable computer storage medium

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Removable_media "Special:EditPage/Removable media") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Removable media"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Removable+media%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Removable+media%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Removable+media%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Removable+media%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Removable+media%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Removable+media%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(September 2014)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

In [computing](/wiki/Computing "Computing"), a **removable media** is a [data storage](/wiki/Computer_data_storage "Computer data storage") [media](/wiki/Storage_media "Storage media") that is designed to be readily inserted and removed from a system.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Most early removable media, such as [floppy disks](/wiki/Floppy_disk "Floppy disk") and [optical discs](/wiki/Optical_disc "Optical disc"), require a dedicated [read](/wiki/Reading_(computer) "Reading (computer)")/write device (i.e. a [drive](/wiki/Disk_drive "Disk drive")) to be installed in the computer,[[2]](#cite_note-2) while others, such as [USB flash drives](/wiki/USB_flash_drive "USB flash drive"), are [plug-and-play](/wiki/Plug-and-play "Plug-and-play") with all the hardware required to read them built into the device, so only need a [driver software](/wiki/Driver_software "Driver software") to be installed in order to communicate with the device.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Some removable media readers/drives are integrated into the [computer case](/wiki/Computer_case "Computer case"), while others are standalone devices that need to be additionally installed or connected.

Examples of removable media that require a dedicated reader drive include:

* [Optical discs](/wiki/Optical_disc "Optical disc"), e.g. [Blu-rays](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") (both standard and [UHD](/wiki/Ultra_HD_Blu-ray "Ultra HD Blu-ray") versions), [DVDs](/wiki/DVD "DVD"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) [CDs](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc")
* [Flash memory](/wiki/Flash_memory "Flash memory")-based [memory cards](/wiki/Memory_card "Memory card"), e.g. [CompactFlash](/wiki/CompactFlash "CompactFlash"), [Secure Digital](/wiki/Secure_Digital "Secure Digital"), [Memory Stick](/wiki/Memory_Stick "Memory Stick")
* Magnetic storage media
  + [Floppy](/wiki/Floppy_disk "Floppy disk") and [Zip](/wiki/Zip_drive "Zip drive") disks (now obsolete)
  + [Disk packs](/wiki/Disk_pack "Disk pack") (now obsolete)
  + [Magnetic tapes](/wiki/Magnetic_tape_data_storage "Magnetic tape data storage")
* [Paper data storage](/wiki/Paper_data_storage "Paper data storage"), e.g. [punched cards](/wiki/Punched_card "Punched card"), [punched tapes](/wiki/Punched_tape "Punched tape") (now obsolete)

Examples of removable media that are standalone [plug-and-play](/wiki/Plug-and-play "Plug-and-play") devices that carry their own reader hardwares include:

* [USB flash drives](/wiki/USB_flash_drive "USB flash drive")[[5]](#cite_note-5)
* [Portable storage devices](/wiki/Portable_storage_device "Portable storage device")
  + Dedicated external [solid-state drives](/wiki/Solid-state_drive "Solid-state drive") (SSD)
  + [Enclosured](/wiki/Disk_enclosure "Disk enclosure") [mass storage](/wiki/Mass_storage "Mass storage") drives, i.e. modified [hard disk drives](/wiki/Hard_disk_drive "Hard disk drive") (HDD)/internal SSDs
* [Peripheral devices](/wiki/Peripheral_device "Peripheral device") that have integrated data storage capability
  + [Digital cameras](/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera")
  + [Mobile devices](/wiki/Mobile_device "Mobile device") such as [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone"), [tablets](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer") and [handheld game consoles](/wiki/Handheld_game_console "Handheld game console")
  + [Portable media players](/wiki/Portable_media_player "Portable media player")
* Other external or dockable peripherals that have expandable removable media capabilities, usually via a [USB port](/wiki/USB_port "USB port") or [memory card reader](/wiki/Memory_card_reader "Memory card reader")
  + [USB hubs](/wiki/USB_hub "USB hub")
  + Wired or wireless [printers](/wiki/Printer_(computing) "Printer (computing)")
  + [Network routers](/wiki/Network_router "Network router"), [access points](/wiki/Wireless_access_point "Wireless access point") and [switches](/wiki/Network_switch "Network switch")

Using removable media can pose some [computer security](/wiki/Computer_security "Computer security") risks, including [viruses](/wiki/Computer_virus "Computer virus"), [data theft](/wiki/Data_theft "Data theft") and the introduction of [malware](/wiki/Malware "Malware").[[6]](#cite_note-NCSC_10_steps-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Removable_media&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The earliest form of removable media, [punched cards](/wiki/Punched_cards "Punched cards") and [tapes](/wiki/Punched_tape "Punched tape"), predates the electronic computer by centuries, with the [Jacquard loom](/wiki/Jacquard_loom "Jacquard loom") of 1801 using interlinked cards to control the machine.[[7]](#cite_note-7) This followed a loom made by [Basile Bouchon](/wiki/Basile_Bouchon "Basile Bouchon") in 1725 that used paper tape for its instructions.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Punched tape was later used in [Colossus](/wiki/Colossus_computer "Colossus computer"), the first electronic computer.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[Magnetic tape](/wiki/Magnetic_tape "Magnetic tape") was developed in the early 20th century in Germany, based on [magnetic wire recording](/wiki/Magnetic_wire_recording "Magnetic wire recording") invented by [Valdemar Poulsen](/wiki/Valdemar_Poulsen "Valdemar Poulsen") in 1898.[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 1951, the [UNIVAC I](/wiki/UNIVAC_I "UNIVAC I") was released, using magnetic tape to store data.[[11]](#cite_note-11) 8-inch [floppy disks](/wiki/Floppy_disk "Floppy disk") were commercially introduced in 1971 by [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM"),[[12]](#cite_note-12) with them being compacted to 5+1⁄4-inch by Shugart Associates in 1976.[[13]](#cite_note-13) At the same time [Compact Cassettes](/wiki/Compact_Cassette "Compact Cassette") started being used to store data, being popular in the late 1970s and 1980s for holding data for [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computers "Personal computers").[[14]](#cite_note-14) In 1982, the 3+1⁄2-inch floppy disk became commonplace, with its introduction for the [Apple Macintosh](/wiki/Apple_Macintosh "Apple Macintosh") and [Amiga](/wiki/Amiga "Amiga").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The [CD-ROM](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM") was introduced in 1985, providing much higher capacity than a floppy disk, however could not be written to. This was resolved in 1990 with the introduction of the [CD-R](/wiki/CD-R "CD-R").[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) The [CD-RW](/wiki/CD-RW "CD-RW"), introduced in 1997 allowed the CD to be written to multiple times, rather than just once, as with the CD-R.[[18]](#cite_note-18) [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") versions of these formats introduced in the late 1990s provided further increases in capacity.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Additional capacity improvements were achieved with [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") in 2006.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The turn of the millennium saw the widespread introduction of [solid-state](/wiki/Solid-state_storage "Solid-state storage") removable media, with the [SD card](/wiki/SD_card "SD card") being introduced in 1999, followed by the [USB flash drive](/wiki/USB_flash_drive "USB flash drive") in 2000.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The capacity of these removable flash drives improved over time, with 2013 seeing [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston_Technology "Kingston Technology") unveiling a 1 [terabyte](/wiki/Terabyte "Terabyte") USB flash drive.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Floppy disk storage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Removable_media&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Floppy disk storage")]

Storage is intended to be used to access data that is access less often. Memory on the other hand is used to access data that is used on a more frequent basis. One of the problems with the earliest computers was how to store data. In the 1950s the [International Business Machines](/wiki/IBM "IBM") (IBM) was trying to solve this problem.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The first floppy disk was developed under the supervision of [Alan Shugart](/wiki/Alan_Shugart "Alan Shugart") in the late 1960s. The floppy disk was not introduced to the public until the 1970s by IBM. But, like any man-made product the floppy disk came with its pros and cons, such as it being cheap and portable while also having severely limited data storage.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Risks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Removable_media&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Risks")]

The use of removable media creates a risk of data loss in the event that the media is lost or stolen. This can lead to the compromise of large amounts of sensitive data, which can result in significant damage to the reputation of a business, as well as possible financial penalties.[[24]](#cite_note-MetaCompliance-24) This risk can be minimised by company policies that reduce the use of removable media when transporting and storing data, and by encrypting the contents of removable media. Prior to the disposal or reuse of removable media, appropriate steps should be taken to ensure that all data previously stored on the device is not accessible.[[6]](#cite_note-NCSC_10_steps-6) Additionally, any device holding sensitive data should be kept secure when not in use.[[24]](#cite_note-MetaCompliance-24)

Removable media can also be used as a [vector](/wiki/Vector_(malware) "Vector (malware)") for malware. Attackers generally use [social engineering](/wiki/Social_engineering_(security) "Social engineering (security)") to get someone to put a media device into a computer, for example by leaving an infected drive in a busy location, from which someone may pick up the device and put it into their computer to find its contents, such an attack is known as baiting. Once the device has been plugged in it can be used to infect an entire network. One example of malware that propagates through removable media such as USB flash drives is [Stuxnet](/wiki/Stuxnet "Stuxnet"), which caused significant damage to [Iran's nuclear program](/wiki/Iran%27s_nuclear_program "Iran's nuclear program"). The risk from an attack like this can be reduced by automatically scanning media for malware and prohibiting users from attempting to access the contents of removable media of unknown origin.[[6]](#cite_note-NCSC_10_steps-6)[[24]](#cite_note-MetaCompliance-24)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Removable_media&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Disk enclosure](/wiki/Disk_enclosure "Disk enclosure")
* [Secure Digital card](/wiki/SD_card "SD card") (SD card)
